data.title: 3 Winners From Micron's Huge Capex Guide
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Last week was always going to be a big week for Nvidia (NVDA) and its ecosystem with the GTC26 event, but also for Micron (MU), which is one of the essential memory suppliers for Nvidia’s chips.

That’s because Micron would also be reporting its Q2 FY26 quarter a couple of days after Nvidia’s GTC event.

Micron’s shares should have catapulted higher on blowout numbers in its Q2 ER, but markets clearly used those earnings results to create winners, and Micron’s capex was the trigger for markets.

Markets believe some WFE (wafer equipment) manufacturers/vendors will become big beneficiaries of Micron’s surging capex over the next year as shown in the chart below.

Exhibit A: Quarterly revenue growth rates for Micron since 1998.

I believe markets are right, and I explain below why Lam Research (NASDAQ:LRCX), Applied Materials (NASDAQ:AMAT), and KLA Corp. (NASDAQ:KLAC) will benefit from Micron’s rising capex as well as elevated capex plans from Micron’s direct peers.

Primer On Micron’s Capex Plans Vs. The Memory Industry

Markets have clearly demonstrated their disapproval for Micron’s forward-looking capex forecasts, as seen in Exhibit A above.

A few days ago, I published this post explaining key details about Micron’s capex components. In my strong view, understanding this nuance about the memory maker’s capex is essential for investors when it comes to investing in WFE vendors.

As I noted in that post, there are 3 components to the capex budgets of most memory makers like Micron—WFE equipment, construction, and test/assembly. Micron’s management usually guides investors on its capex plans in dollar terms and the areas /components that management intends to spend on.

Markets are very appreciative of management when the focus on Micron’s capex is/will be directly towards WFE to support the production of its advanced memory products/components, especially for HBM and next-gen DRAM nodes. Spending on EUV (extreme ultraviolet) lithography is one example of WFE capex, which goes directly into developing more advanced technology manufacturing process nodes like the 1-γ node for DRAM and the G9 node for 3D NAND.

However, on the Q2 call, Micron’s management guided up FY26 capex to $25B, up from prior estimates of $18B, and simultaneously noted that this 39% sequential increase in the FY26 capex outlook would be “driven by cleanroom facility-related CapEx,” like the Tonglou, Taiwan location. This would be “followed by a construction spend increase in our U.S. fab projects" in FY26.

That’s not all. Management also revealed construction capex would see a ~$10B increase in FY27:

We project our fiscal 2027 CapEx to step up meaningfully to support HBM- and DRAM-related investments. We expect construction-related CapEx to increase by over $10 billion year-over-year in fiscal 2027 as we build out our global manufacturing sites to address long-term demand opportunities. In addition, we expect higher equipment spend year-over-year in fiscal 2027.

This clearly demarcates some key beneficiaries from not just Micron’s elevated focus on construction capex but also from rising construction-focused capex from Micron’s peers like Samsung and SK Hynix.

Here are my winners from the elevated construction-focused capex being seen among the three largest memory makers in the world, as shown below.

1. Lam Research: Strong Support For Cleanrooms

Such is the strength in the WFE space that Lam Research’s management believes the entire WFE industry’s TAM can grow 22% in CY26 to $135B. These growth estimates are still likely conservative since they see a “shortage of available clean room space.”

Typically, Lam Research is known to provide products and solutions that help chip foundries like Micron during initial chip fabrication stages of deposition and etching. Lam Research is most known for its Systems division, which houses its deposition and etching solutions.

But during the stages of setting up clean rooms, like I talked about in the previous section, a large volume of resources are allocated towards constructing chip fabrication shells. Lam Research engages chip foundries early on during the construction stages in areas like facility planning, WFE transport, and advanced WFE installation. And Lam Research engages customers like Micron and peers via its Customer Support business group, which brought in $1.68B in the December quarter. This business group began accelerating sequentially, growing 13.5% in December, versus the sluggish growth through Q2 and Q3 quarters.

This all-round growing strength in Lam Research’s business made management up their outlook at a recent conference this month, where they said end demand “is stronger than” the prior $135B forecast, underlining a prior outlook that H2 CY26 will be strong for the company as Lam Research benefits from elevated construction capex.

2. Applied Materials: AGS Growth Vector

I have written about Applied Materials before and switched from being bullish to neutral. But that was 15 months ago, so a refresh to Applied Materials is needed.

Just like Lam Research, Applied Materials expects a strong pickup in growth in H2 this year, and a shortage of enough cleanrooms will be “a key factor pacing the rate of [WFE] investment,” according to management.

Applied Materials has two key revenue segments, Semiconductor Systems and AGS (Applied Global Services), that each account for ~47-48% of the overall business. Via AGS, Applied Materials engages with customers by providing on-field site engineers to help design, plan, and support WFE setup and installation. Customers also use Applied Materials' AIx tool for virtual fab mapping and create interactive digital twins of the new foundry layout, allowing customers like Micron, Samsung, and SK Hynix to simulate WFE placement, maximize clean room space, and remove potential bottlenecks before WFEs and machines are physically installed.

3. KLA Corp: Semi Process Control

Unlike Lam Research and Applied Materials, KLA Corp focuses on providing semiconductor process control and process-enabling solutions. Process control solutions like its inspection, quality control, and chip yield management solutions account for 90% or more of its revenues.

As new cleanroom facilities are being set up or existing facilities are being extended for more capacity, KLA’s tools are typically deployed to monitor the cleanliness of the new fabrication environment, ensuring that air quality and foundry environmental conditions meet standards set by customers like Micron before the WFE is installed and during the operation of WFE. KLA also deploys their onsite engineers routinely on behalf of the customer at the customer fab site to support the installation of advanced process control as expensive WFE from companies like ASML get installed in these cleanrooms.

The big difference between KLA and the other two peers that I mentioned above is that KLA operates at strong gross margins that stand apart from Applied Materials and Lam Research, which makes KLA Corp. a lot more interesting in this era of elevated construction capex seen among broader foundry companies and memory companies.

Recently, KLA Corp extended their current share buyback program from the $3.9B that still exists by an incremental $7B. The total value of their buyback program represents ~5% of KLA’s market cap.

Valuations May Be Stretched But Don’t Factor Coming Upside

All three WFE companies follow Micron’s FY system that ends in August. That means all three

The shares of all three capex beneficiaries I noted above have already run up in anticipation of the coming capex boom from their target customers. This can be seen in elevated forward revenue multiples below.

Lam Research is the only company that analysts believe can score 21-23% growth in FY26 and FY27. Compared to forward growth, Lam Research trades at 13x FY26 revenues and 11x FY27 revenues.

In contrast to Lam Research, its other large peer, Applied Materials is expected to grow 11% in FY26 and 18% in FY27 but trades at 9.4x FY26 and just 8x FY27 revenues.

Finally, KLA, the smallest of the three, in terms of market cap, trades at 15x FY26 revenues and 12.7x FY27 revenues for revenue growth projections of 10% this FY that more than doubles to 21.7% in FY27.

Personally, I believe all three companies are still undervalued because there is still strong scope for revenue estimates to keep increasing. For example, FY27 revenue growth estimates for all three companies were hiked by 10-15% in just the last 3 months as per SA data. So there is definitely more upside in the shares of KLA, Lam Research, and Applied Materials.

Risks & Other Factors To Note

Two main risks when investing in the WFE companies I noted in this post.

First, all companies have some amount of revenue exposure to China, and that introduces the risk of possible regulatory oversight and the added vulnerability of tariffs/export restrictions that could return.

Two, Applied Materials, Lam Research, and KLA are highly sensitive to the capex budgets of their target customers, which introduces a different kind of business cyclicality and capex volatility. So if their customers suddenly see any weakness in their own businesses, they may scale back capex, which may hurt all three beneficiaries.

Takeaway

Micron’s ballooning capex is a direct reaction function to the unprecedented demand environment seen in the memory market.

While that benefits Micron long-term, the new capacity that Micron and its peers are rushing to create will make strong beneficiaries out of WFE vendors such as Applied Materials, KLA Corp., and Lam Research that are best positioned to assist each foundry in the global race to construct more clean rooms.

I am bullish on the shares of Applied Materials, KLA Corp and Lam Research.

data.title: No Need To Rush Into Arrow Financial
data.description:
Just a couple of days ago, I finished an analysis of Greene County Bancorp (GCBC). I rated the stock as a Buy based on its strong earnings growth, credit quality, and operational metrics. My research piqued my curiosity about other small-cap banks in the area and led me to learn more about Arrow Financial Corporation (AROW). With a gain of over 22% in the last 12 months, AROW has certainly made some meaningful improvements to its bottom line in the last year that have caught the market’s attention. Its Q4 2025 results included a record net income of $14 million, or $0.85 per share.

A deeper dive into the company’s operations, however, paints a noticeable contrast to many of the positives that I saw in GCBC. Several of Arrow's key barometers for performance fall short of the usual standards I want to see in a small-cap bank before deeming it a Buy. The company also recently made an offer to acquire privately held Adirondack Bancorp in a deal that is valued at about $89 million. Reading through the terms of the deal and the assets that AROW will acquire did not spark any new enthusiasm on my end. I consider Arrow Financial to be a Hold for the reasons discussed below.

Company Overview

With a geographic footprint of 38 branches in Northeast New York, Arrow Financial is the holding company for Arrow Bank. Formerly operating under the names Glens Falls National Bank and Trust Company and Saratoga National Bank and Trust Company, Arrow is a full-service commercial bank that also offers wealth management and insurance services to its communities. In 2024, it was named to Piper Sandler’s Small-Cap All-Stars, a list of high-quality banks that included Greene County Bancorp in 2025.

AROW has a current market cap of $550.2 million. According to its 2025 10-K filing, the company controlled $4.45 billion in assets at the end of last year, including a loan portfolio worth $3.45 billion. The bank’s total gross loans only grew by 1.7% in FY 2025.

Further detail regarding the company’s loan mix can be seen below. Unlike many other small regional banks that are heavily exposed to commercial real estate, Arrow’s largest loan allocation belongs to home mortgages, which make up 40% of the total portfolio. Consumer loans are the next largest segment at approximately 31%. These loans are primarily auto loans that were originated through dealerships in New York and Vermont. I am generally less enthusiastic about investing in companies with high exposure to auto loan debt, as delinquency rates are often about twice what they are for home mortgages. New York State has an auto loan delinquency rate of 4.8%, which places it in the middle of the pack compared to other states in the US.

Total deposits held by the bank were valued at $3.94 billion at the end of last year. This was a 2.9% increase from the end of 2024. Non-interest-bearing deposits comprised $722 million, or 18.3%, of that most recent total. An additional $850 million was held in interest-bearing checking accounts that paid less than 1% in interest in Q4. The bank’s overall cost of deposits is relatively in line with the national average for a bank of its size.

Arrow Financial currently pays a quarterly dividend of $0.30 per share. The company has recorded eight straight years of dividend growth. AROW is on its way to nine years of higher payouts after increasing its distribution in Q1 2026 by 3.4%. The stock has a forward yield of 3.6%.

Analysis Of Arrow Financial

As mentioned in my introduction, Q4 2025 was a record quarter for net income for Arrow Financial, and that strong bottom-line performance helped the company achieve earnings per share of $2.65 for FY 2025. Net income of $44 million for the full year was 48.1% better than the $29.7 million earned in FY 2024. The biggest driver in this improvement was an increase in net interest income of $21.4 million over the last 12 months. Loan growth and higher rates charged on those loans helped increase interest income while the bank was paying lower interest on deposits as savings rates declined.

Even as these trends were certainly all positive for the bank, AROW is still below average in its net interest margin. The bank’s NIM of 3.24% is impacted by the higher rates paid on municipal deposits. As shown in the chart below that details all of the funding sources for Arrow Bank, $787 million, or 19.8%, of the bank’s deposits are from municipal depositors.

Like its net interest margin, other performance metrics for the bank are just below or right around average when compared to similar-sized banks. AROW’s return on assets in Q4 2025 was 1.24%, a significant improvement from Q4 2024, when the number stood at just 0.41%. During that final quarter of the previous year, the company booked a number of non-recurring charges, including a $3 million loss on the sale of investment securities. Arrow Financial’s efficiency ratio was 59.0% at the end of Q4 2025, 42 basis points higher than its peer mean. Again, this number has been showing improvement, which would lead me to be more optimistic about the year ahead.

One statistic that has not been improving for the bank is its share of delinquent loans. As the graph below shows, the ratio of delinquencies to total loans has been increasing each quarter since Q1 2025. It ended the year at 0.84%, its highest level in four years. Net charge-offs at the end of last year were 0.19%, which is 20 basis points better than average but 10 basis points higher than at the end of 2024. Non-performing loans stood at just 0.24% at the end of the year, and the bank’s allowance of credit losses coverage seemed more than able to manage any problems in the short term.

Overall, my assessment of Arrow Financial as a stand-alone entity is that the company is showing good momentum in some areas but still has room for improvement. Its stock trades at a forward P/E of 9.1, which seems reasonable for its operations and recent performance. The company's tangible book value per share of $24.71 was a 10.3% improvement from the end of 2024. Its stock currently trades at a price-to-TBV ratio of 1.35. Without the addition of its soon-to-be-acquired assets, I put Arrow Financial in the "good, but not great" category for small-cap regional banks.

Analysis Of Adirondack Bancorp Acquisition

Adirondack Bancorp is the holding company for Adirondack Bank, which operates 19 branch locations and one additional loan production office in Upstate and Central New York. The company reported just $942 million in assets at the end of 2025 after crossing over the $1 billion threshold at the end of Q3 2025. A pro forma look at what the combination of Adirondack and Arrow could look like, both from a geographical and financial standpoint, is given below.

On the surface, the geographical additions from the acquisition may be more valuable than the actual assets. Micron Technology recently started the construction of a chip-making plant just north of Syracuse that could add as many as 9000 jobs in the region. Last April, yogurt producer Chobani broke ground on a $1.2 billion plant in Rome, NY, that could create an additional 1000 jobs. News items like these give hope for economic expansion in the region that could benefit the newly acquired branches from Adirondack Bank.

From the standpoint of its current operations, Adirondack looks somewhat less appealing. The bank’s return on assets and return on equity ratios, which were at 0.52% and 6.97%, respectively, at the end of Q4 2025, are well below the average for a small community bank. Its most recently reported net interest margin was two basis points below the national average of 3.96%. Adirondack also has a Tier 1 leverage ratio of 8.54%, which is below the 9% standard that I would prefer in a bank of this size.

The pro forma loan and deposit data in the graphic below show the impact of the deal on Arrow Financial. Its yield on loans, which was below average, will increase slightly from 5.47% to 5.50%. Arrow’s high concentration in residential mortgages will grow even more as 47.8% of Adirondack’s loan mix was concentrated in this area. Its cost of deposits will decline 21 basis points as Adirondack has a remarkably low percentage in this area due in large part to 36.5% of its deposits being held in non-interest-bearing accounts.

The deal is being done at a price-to-tangible book value multiple of 1.38, only slightly below the average valuation of all banks in the US but just above the valuation for AROW. Once completed, Arrow Financial is expected to see its own TBV drop by 9.1%, with the earn-back requiring almost three years of time. Arrow estimates that the benefits from the combination of operations will result in an ROA of 1.4% by FY 2027 and a drop in its efficiency rate to 55.1%. The transaction is expected to close in late Q2 2026 or early Q3.

Risks To Consider

Even though the deal for Adirondack Bancorp is relatively small, there are still risks associated with the transaction. The odds of the deal not going through are minimal given the lenient regulatory environment for banks in the US, but that chance does still exist. If the deal is finalized as scheduled, there is the chance that the acquisition will not be as beneficial to Arrow Financial as expected. Growth for the current Adirondack branches may suffer as customers take the merger as an opportunity to shop their accounts with competing banks. The deal will also add about $40 million of goodwill to AROW’s balance sheet.

The second risk to consider before investing in Arrow Financial is its limited geographic diversity within the state of New York. Even after folding in the branches from Adirondack Bancorp, the company will be heavily dependent on the state of New York—its job growth, regulatory environment, and tax policy. New York, like several other high-tax states, is grappling with issues of resident migration to other lower-tax states. Even if New York can keep pace with competing states, the entire financial sector could be in for an extended pullback due to the impact of higher oil prices and higher-than-expected overall inflation in the first half of this year. Just last week, Moody’s top economist stated that the chances of a recession in the US in the next 12 months now stand at 49%.

Seeking Alpha’s Quant rating currently has a Sell rating on AROW. The stock does receive a “B” in growth, based largely on the earnings improvements that have taken place over the last year. However, it has a “C” in valuation and a failing grade in revisions after the one analyst that covers the company decreased its earnings estimate for FY 2026. I am not one to place too much stock in estimates that are only connected to one single analyst, but this lower estimate was released in February, which could link its cause to the announcement of the Adirondack deal.

The Bottom Line

It is quite possible that the addition of Adirondack to Arrow's operations will be beneficial in the long term, but I do not think it provides justification for a Buy recommendation at this time. My analysis of Arrow by itself is that is it a decent bank, but not a standout. In short, I see no impending catalyst that will cause AROW to perform better than the average small-cap regional bank.

The added stress in the markets that could come from higher inflation, a slowing job market, and the declining odds of a Fed rate cut in the near term could make it more challenging for any regional bank to show meaningful gains in the first half of this year. The 3.6% dividend yield may give some reward to investors who want to hold and wait through the turbulence. However, in my opinion, it is not enough to recommend a buy at this time.

data.title: American Tower: Tenant Default Creates Dip-Buying Opportunity - Diversified 5G/AI Prospects
data.description:
I previously covered American Tower Corporation (AMT) in December 2025, discussing why I had reiterated my Buy rating then, thanks to the excellent margin of safety arising from the steep correction/discounted valuations, the reasonable technical indicators, and the robust dual pronged return prospects across capital appreciation/dividend incomes.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am reiterating my Buy rating for the AMT stock here, thanks to the mixed optics from the tenant default/underwhelming FY2026 guidance already triggering the stock price selloff and the consequently cheap valuations/richer dividend yields.

My optimism is further aided by the REIT's diversified growth/monetization tailwinds across the legacy telecom and data center opportunities, during the multi-year 5G/6G/AI-related boom.

AMT's Tenant Headwinds Discussed

Since my last Buy rating, AMT has missed the great market rotation in December 2025, with the telecom/data center REIT seemingly left in the dust, similar to another pure-play telecom REIT, Crown Castle Inc. (CCI).

Part of AMT's headwinds may be attributed to the prior default of DISH Wireless, a tenant that comprises 2% of the REIT's consolidated property revenue and 4% of the UCAN property revenues in FY2025, with it directly contributing to the REIT's impacted FY2026 guidance as the management "continue to pursue legal action to recover the value of its remaining lease obligations."

For reference, AMT has offered an underwhelming FY2026 guidance at:

  • the consolidated organic tenant billings growth at approximately +2% YoY,
  • the adj EBITDA growth at approximately -0.1% YoY,
  • the AFFO per share growth at approximately +1% YoY,

against the FY2025 outperformance at +5.1% YoY, +4.7% YoY, and +8% YoY, respectively.

When compared to AMT's historical top/bottom-line (AFFO per share) growth profile at a 5Y CAGR of +5.8%/+5.9% and 10Y CAGR of +8.4%/+7.8%, respectively, I can understand why the market has opted to de-risk first as the stock lost -7.1% of its value post FQ4'25 earnings call.

Otherwise, I am of the opinion that the selloff has been unwarranted, since on an adj basis (after discounting DISH's default), the REIT already expects to deliver a robust adj FY2026 performance at +5% YoY, +5% YoY, and +5% YoY, respectively, with it apparent that the default merely presents a temporary noise in their performance metric.

AMT's Robust 5G/Data Center Monetization Prospects Discussed

1. Telecom/5G/6G Prospects

This is especially since AMT's ongoing capex investment at 2,000 new tower sites is expected to be accretive to its top-line performance by +1% in overall organic tenant billings growth in FY2026.

Given that the global mobile data traffic is expected to grow from 196.7 exabytes per month in 2025 to 483.2 exabytes per month in 2031, expanding at an accelerated CAGR of +16.6%, I am the opinion that its prospects as a leading telecom REIT player is likely to be extremely robust indeed, given the required "doubling in wireless network capacity between now and 2030."

Part of the demand tailwinds are attributed to the increased use of AI at the edge (smart devices) along with the upcoming 5G capacity investments in the EU and emerging international countries - all of which are expected to drive higher mobile data consumption moving forward.

This is on top of the next decade's 6G opportunities, as analysts posited 2028 as "the year of significant advancements, revolutionizing connectivity with unprecedented bandwidth, lightning-fast speed, and ultra-low latency," with it lending credibility to the management's guidance of higher tower activities and organic growth opportunities over the next few years.

2. Data Center Prospects

At the same time, readers cannot forget AMT's double-digit growth prospects from the data center segment, with the robust demand for interconnection-rich colocation deployments already driving the revenue growth by +19% YoY in FQ4'25 (page 31) and the notably richer operating profit margin at 57.2% (+8.4 points YoY).

This is thanks to the accretive capex trends, as observed in the expanding ending total portfolio at 317 MW in FQ4'25 (+16.1% YoY) and the higher Monthly Recurring Revenue per Cabinet Equivalent Billed at $1,989 (+8.7% YoY).

With AMT already guiding data center revenue growth by +12.5% YoY in FY2026 against the +13.9% reported in FY2025 and the segment notably being a growth driver, it is unsurprising that they have allocated a higher capex at $695M in FY2026 (+4.4% YoY) with the new 55 MW under construction likely to be top/bottom-line accretive upon energization.

At the same time, there remains a great, multi-year growth runway, as observed in the 279 MW held for future development, allowing them to tap into the cloud super cycle through the expanded capacities/higher rates as similarly observed in the management's guidance of "CoreSite's achievement of mid-teens or higher stabilized yields on new data center deployments."

Profitable Growth Trends At Discounted Valuations

As a result of the robust FY2025 performance and the excellent adj FY2026 outlook, it is unsurprising that the consensus forward estimates remain promising, with AMT expected to report an excellent top/bottom-line growth prospect (FFO per share) at a CAGR of +3.6%/+7.9% through FY2028, respectively.

At the same time, the management has also hinted at their ability to drive further tower cash EBITDA margin expansion by up to 300 basis points through 2030, thanks to:

  • the planned land optimization/maintenance care of their international assets,
  • the implementation of a global, unified supply chain for an improved economy of scale, and
  • the streamlined technological platforms to "optimize customer service and accelerate automation,"

with it further lending credibility to their consistently rich adj EBITDA margins of 67% in FY2025 (in line YoY/+4 points from the 63% reported in FY2019).

Thanks to the recent correction, AMT looks even more compelling at FWD Price/FFO valuations of 18.05x and FWD Price/AFFO valuations of 16.22x, down from the 1Y mean of 19.66x/18.42x, the 5Y mean of 21.56x/21.48x, and the 5Y pre-pandemic mean of 21.50x/19.99x, respectively.

When comparing AMT's discounted FWD Price/FFO valuations of 18.05x and the consensus FFO per share growth estimates at a 3Y CAGR of +7.9% to its direct peers, including:

  • pure-play telecom REIT - CCI at 20.99x/+4.8%,
  • pure-play Data Center REIT - Digital Realty Trust (DLR) at 22.19x/+8.7%,
  • mixed Data Center/Storage REIT - Iron Mountain (IRM) at 25.77x/+11.4%,
  • pure-play Data Center REIT - Equinix, Inc. (EQIX) at 30.58x/+11.5%, respectively,

I am of the opinion that AMT's discounted valuations against the relatively robust FFO per share growth prospects have presented interested investors with a great dip buying opportunity here.

Readers must not ignore AMT's stable share count on a YoY basis and the relatively healthy net debt to adj EBITDA ratio of 4.9x in FY2025 as well, compared to CCI at 8.34x, EQIX at 3.8x, IRM at 4.9x, and DLR at 4.9x, with it apparent that AMT continues to offer a compelling investment thesis.

These metrics position AMT well for their return to "accelerating, durable growth in 2027 and beyond," made sweeter by the recently raised dividends by +5.2%, the secure payouts at AFFO payout ratio of 65.62% (against the sector median of 74.07%), and the still rich yields at 4.05% compared to recent lows of 3.52% (against the sector median of 5.03%).

AMT Is An Even Better Buy Here

For now, the bulls have consistently defended AMT's 2024/2025/2026 trading floor of $170s, with it presenting great insights for those looking to add, especially since the pessimism embedded in its valuations is partly attributed to the DISH default.

Based on the FWD Price/AFFO valuations of 16.22x and the FY2025 AFFO per share performance at $10.76 (+8% YoY), it is apparent that the stock is still trading near to my fair value estimates of $174.50.

Based on the FWD Price/FFO valuations of 18.05x and the consensus FY2028 FFO per share estimates of $11.16, there remains a more than decent upside potential of +14.1% to my long-term, AMT price target of $201.40 as well.

This is not even taking into account the potential upgrading in its valuations nearer to the 5Y pre-pandemic Price/FFO valuation mean of 21.50x (still cheap compared to its peer group mean of 23.50x), potentially upon the recovery in market sentiments post DISH default, with it likely to trigger an expanded upside potential of +35.9% to my bull-case long-term price target of $239.90.

Combined with the still compelling/secure dividend investment thesis as discussed above, the management's confidence about delivering their prior organic growth rate at approximately +5% through 2027, and the great insights from the established trading floor at the $170s, I am reiterating my Buy rating for AMT stock here.

Do not miss this dip.

data.title: Lionsgate: Will Continue To Roar
data.description:
In May 2024, the old Lions Gate Entertainment sold a ~12% stake in Lionsgate Studios (LION) to a SPAC for $10/share. In May 2025, Lions Gate distributed to shareholders one share of LION for every share of Lions Gate Entertainment they held. LION owns the movie and television production studios as well as the over 20,000 title film and television library, which includes the John Wick, Hunger Games, Twilight, Now You See Me, Knives Out, Mad Men, Ghosts etc.

Since the spin, LION has traded as low as $5.55 and as high as $11.02 recently, but generally below that $10 investment by the SPAC. Importantly, after the May 2025 spin, Liberty Strategic Capital (led by former Treasury Secretary Steve Mnuchin) increased its stake to 12.6% via open-market purchases. I believe these actions were one reason the company adopted a poison pill that expires in May of this year. Liberty bought more shares in Q3 to bring his stake to 12.94%, and in January, entered into a standstill arrangement whereby he would not buy more than 17.5% of the company for one year and Mnuchin was named to the Board of Directors. MHR, run by former Lions Gate Chairman Mark Rachevsky, owns 12.97%. Rachevsky is no longer on the board.

I first started looking at LION during the split from STRZ. (I wrote about STRZ for subscribers of my Investing Group service.) The Warner Brothers (WBD) saga refocused my attention on LION, specifically the value of its library. In my opinion, while Netflix (NFLX) and Paramount (PSKY) found WBD's production assets and the HBOmax streaming service valuable, a major draw was the purported 8,500 title movie and 50,000 title (over 5,000 separate series) television library. These include Warner Bros., HBO, Turner Entertainment (including pre-1986 MGM and other classics), New Line, Castle Rock, Hanna-Barbera and others. In an age where content is king, this library is a huge prize, one PSKY is willing to pay over 15x total EBITDA for.

My thesis was and continues to be that the WBD deal highlights the value embedded in LION. With WBD gone, LION becomes one of the largest if not the largest standalone library. Moreover, I believe it is highly likely that PSKY, the apparent winner of WBD, will not license any of the WBD library outside of the Paramount or HBOmax streaming services. That holdback should increase the value of any LION licenses to other services that need or want content.

Valuation:

WBD does not break out the revenue or profits from its library. LION does. In the most recent earnings release, the company announced that trailing twelve month library revenue was $1.05 billion, up 10% from the prior quarter's TTM results. The company has stated that library EBITDA margins are about 45%. Therefore, TTM library EBITDA was about $475 million. This EBITDA figure is considerably higher than the ~$250 million consensus EBITDA for the whole company for 2026.

I think it is silly to look at quarterly or even annual EBITDA of a movie/television studio's production department. First of all, production accounting is notoriously messy. Companies frontload production costs, which can make one quarter or year look terrible. Then, when the film or show, comes out, profits look artificially high. However, the beauty of this model is that once a movie or a show is a hit, it generates profits for years or decades. For example, it was reported that Netflix signed a five-year licensing deal north of $500 million for Seinfeld. Outside of residuals to writers, actors, etc, that $500 million goes straight to the pockets of the show's owners, Jerry Seinfeld and Larry David.

Therefore, I look at LION solely on the value of its library in an acquisition. I believe any acquirer would likely eliminate most of the corporate overhead and then milk the value of the library. I'm certain the production assets and teams have value, but this valuation method gives them to you for free.

Market Cap (@$9.65) $2.80 billion
Debt $1.922 billion
Minority Interest $144 million
Cash $182 million
Enterprise Value $4.685 billion
EV/TTM Library EBITDA ($475 million) 9.86x

As you can see above, the current value translates to a less than 10x multiple of TTM studio EBITDA. All other assets would be free. If you applied a 12x multiple to the studio EBITDA (a 20% discount to all of WBD), you would get another ~$3.50/share of value for LION (a little over $13/share).

Given the bidding war we just witnessed for WBD, if such a bidding war broke out for LION, 12x for the library sounds like a low multiple for me, particularly given the quality of the library and its recent production nature (85% produced since 2000). Every turn of that $474 million library EBITDA equals about $1.65 on the shares.

Odds of a Bidding War:

Now that the WBD saga has reach the first stage of its denouement with PSKY the apparent winner but not attained regulatory approval, I believe eyes can and might turn to LION. It is a large library of recently produced content that just grew 10% year over year. Unlike WBD, I believe it is also of a size that anti-trust would be a minor issue even for someone like Netflix. It is also small enough that a private equity-backed player like Legendary Films to explore buying the company.

Mnuchin's stake and knowledge of the company and industry makes him a contender to buy the whole business. I believe his presence and open market purchases are reasons why the company adopted the poison pill, which forces any acquirer to negotiate with the board. Should he not intend to purchase the company, Mnuchin's standstill agreement and seat on the board positions him for a say on who does purchase it and the price paid.

Risk:

The main trading risk here is that the WBD deal gets blocked somehow. If WBD falls, LION likely will as well even though the two don't have that much to do with one another. Another risk is operational. LION had some dud films last year and the year before, which hurt studio profits. Lastly, a pandemic or a writers/actors strike would hurt both production and movie attendance.

Conclusion:

I think LION is a unique asset that will become even more unique if WBD is purchased by PSKY. Even without that deal, however, I think the company can benefit from continued growth of its library and the cash that spits off as well and potential success of upcoming releases such as the upcoming Michael Jackson biopic. The shares can and likely will bounce around but I think there is more upside than down over time for the patient investor.

data.title: Winnebago Industries' Ride Lower Offers An Opportunity To Jump In
data.description:
March 25th was a really difficult day for shareholders of recreational vehicle company Winnebago Industries, Inc. (WGO). The stock fell around 6.9% after management announced financial results for the second quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. This was actually surprising to me. Revenue and profitability improved year over year. And in fact, financial performance came in above what analysts were anticipating. The company is targeting some growth this year, both on the top and bottom lines. But what likely turned investors off was the fact that the company believes that market conditions will remain mixed throughout 2026.

In my previous article about the business, published in December of last year, I acknowledged the mixed picture of the business. And in a separate article completely unrelated to Winnebago Industries, I did challenge the narrative that this year will be a positive year for the RV industry. But at the same time, because of how cheap shares are, I have been bullish. Long term, I believe in the company. And even though the stock is down since I last wrote about it, even underperforming the S&P 500 during that time, I believe that it will do fine enough to justify keeping it as a "Buy" candidate.

Taking A Ride With Winnebago Industries

The second quarter of the 2026 fiscal year was actually a good time for Winnebago Industries. Take revenue for starters. It came in at $657.4 million for the quarter. That happened to be 6% above the $620.2 million that the company reported the previous year. It also was $30.2 million above what analysts were hoping to see. This is, objectively, a good outcome for the company. And on the bottom line, we saw strength as well.

Earnings per share, for instance, went from negative $0.02 to positive $0.17. That reading ended up being $0.03 higher than what analysts were hoping to see. It ultimately translated to a rise in the bottom line from a loss of $0.4 million to a profit of $4.8 million. On an adjusted basis, earnings per share improved as well, climbing from $0.19 to $0.27. That ended up being $0.08 higher than what analysts were hoping to see and meant a rise in adjusted net profits from $3.8 million to $7.6 million. Most other profitability metrics also improved. One exception was operating cash flow, which worsened from negative $10.5 million to negative $24.8 million. But if we strip out changes in working capital, we get a jump from $21.9 million to $44.8 million. Finally, EBITDA for the company expanded from $22.8 million to $24.4 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the first half of the 2026 fiscal year compared to the same time in 2025. Revenue, profits, and cash flows improved without exception. This is a testament to the current strength of the company in a market that is undeniably being challenged by high-interest rates and weak consumer confidence. In fact, as the chart below illustrates, consumer confidence levels are near all-time lows. I have argued in other articles, with the furthest dating back to August of last year, that a recession is highly likely to occur in the near term. And I believe that the case for that has strengthened over the last few months. And now, with oil prices flying sky high, the cost of having an RV has also risen. After all, they aren't exactly known for being fuel efficient. Larger RVs, for instance, typically get between 6 and 10 miles per gallon, with larger ones sometimes getting in excess of 20. The average car, meanwhile, gets about 24.4 miles per gallon.

When it comes to financial performance for the business, revenue expanded only because of its Motorhome segment. Sales jumped from $235.6 million to $304.7 million. That resulted in operating income improving from negative $0.6 million to positive $7.5 million. When you dig into the numbers, you can see that the number of motor homes sold by the company shot up from 1,144 to 1,518. This came even as the number of Class A vehicles dropped from 278 to 206. By comparison, the number of Class C units rose from 583 to 670, while the number of Class B units skyrocketed from 283 to 642.

Management stressed that they are continuing to make innovations in this market. When it comes to the Class C category, for instance, the company has been targeting affordability and value accessibility to its premium brands while also introducing new technologies. At the same time as this, it's also pushing for growth in higher-value segments like its Class A diesel, Class C diesel, and Super C categories. These are all premium models that bring in more bang for the buck.

This does not mean that the company has seen strength everywhere. Other parts of the company, for instance, have struggled. Towable revenue, for instance, dropped from $288.2 million to $262.4 million, bringing operating profits down from $12.7 million to $11.1 million. Unfortunately, the company suffered from a decline in the number of towable units from 7,225 to 6,615, with its rise in travel trailers more than offset by a plunge in fifth wheel units of 29.2%.

Management specifically mentioned that they have seen intense competition involving fifth wheels, including not only from legacy competitors but also from newer ones. What's really telling here is that unit sales dropped so much even though management introduced new offerings such as its Omega Frame, which it describes as one of the most durable and strongest frames that exists in the market. The firm also recently developed a composite leakproof roof that is starting to be rolled out across its Grand Design line, starting with its Solitude line on its fifth wheel side of its offerings. To address some of the challenges that the company is facing here, it has been partnering with dealers to right-size the programmatic and promotional support that it has around these models in this environment. But so far, we don't see any real progress here.

The marine side of the business has faced challenges as well. As the number of units sold dropped from 1,046 to 992, revenue declined from $81.7 million to $79.2 million. This was even though its Barletta brand has seen continued market share expansion in the U.S. aluminum pontoon segment, growing to 9.1% this year compared to 8.9% last year. Management has made certain innovations in the space, including the rollout of pontoon-tuned software, a redesigned hull, expanded seating and storage, and more. But still, the decline in revenue occurred, and operating profits fell as a result, declining from $5.4 million to $2.9 million.

When it comes to the 2026 fiscal year, management has forecasted revenue of between $2.80 billion and $3 billion. Even at the low end, that would basically match what the company saw in 2025. And if revenue comes to fruition at the midpoint, adjusted earnings per share will likely translate to adjusted net profits of about $69.8 million. That stacks up nicely compared to the $47.2 million that the company reported for 2025. This would almost certainly mean a rise in adjusted operating cash flow to $175.7 million and an increase in EBITDA to $178.9 million.

This rise in revenue is almost certainly because of slightly bullish expectations when it comes to the industry more broadly. In the chart above, you can see historical RV shipments over the last several years. For 2025, the industry saw shipments amounting to 342,220 units. That did represent a slight increase over what was seen the year before. The RV Industry Association (or RVIA for short) believes that industry shipments this year could be anywhere from 328,800 to 367,000. The midpoint there of 347,900 would represent only a modest increase compared to what was seen last year. Management, for its part, seems to believe that market conditions will remain mixed.

They cited soft retail demand in the marine segment, as well as dealer inventory management issues across both the RV and marine markets. Broader geopolitical conflict, as well as macroeconomic issues, such as those that I mentioned already, are top of mind. But management is working to cut costs while also selectively changing prices and working to be proactive with inventories and capital to address these concerns. Personally, I think that industry conditions will end up being weaker than expected. Already, RVIA data has been revised lower. The midpoint of expectations earlier this year for the 2025 calendar year was 345,000 units being sold for the industry. And the midpoint for 2026 was 352,500. The fact that these numbers are now lower is telling that industry conditions are proving more challenging than what even industry experts have indicated. And as economic conditions deteriorate further, we could see some pain.

Even if the company does not see any improvement from a profitability standpoint, I would argue that it makes for an attractive prospect. In the chart above, you can see how the company is valued using results from 2025 and estimates for 2026. It's naturally more attractive on a forward basis. But even if we see financial performance match what the company saw last year, it is still priced at levels that are relatively appealing. In that same chart, you can see how the business compares to five other similar firms. Only two of them ended up being cheaper than it is on both a price-to-earnings basis and on an EV-to-EBITDA basis. Meanwhile, on a price-to-operating-cash-flow basis, only one of these companies was cheaper.

Takeaway

I would say that the market is being rational in reacting to potentially softer conditions in the RV industry for this year. Having said that, this is a clear value play with a management team that knows what they are doing. While the stock has disappointed in recent months, trading down 22.7% since I last wrote about it, I remain confident in the long-term vision that exists here. At the end of the day, this justifies, in my view, a bullish outlook. And that is why I don't mind maintaining it as a "Buy" candidate.

data.title: Why I Remain Constructive On U.S. Markets
data.description:
Why did the S&P 500 "SPX" (SP500) and the Nasdaq 100 (NDX) futures surge by approximately 4% in pre-market on Monday morning? And why did Oil prices crater?

Just one "Tweet" about negotiating with Iran turned the bearish sentiment upside down, sending stocks surging, at least in the near term. So, it appears that the market may trust in Trump after all.

Now, I've actually gotten more near-term cautious on markets in recent months, well before the conflict with Iran "broke out." In early February, I put out this cautionary correction article, when the market was around its peak.

In fact, I began questioning the market's health in the short-term as soon as Kevin Warsh was nominated to lead the Fed, and we witnessed unprecedented crashes in gold, silver, as well as in other assets. I also discussed my "five-step" plan for the upcoming months in my early February correction article highlighted above, as we had entered a period of heightened market uncertainty and increased volatility.

My Five Step Correction Plan Process

Just to reiterate, the plan was:

1. Reduce risk exposure first and foremost, to avoid considerable drawdowns, especially in the riskier pockets of equity and other risk asset markets.

2. Increase cash and get out of margin to decrease risk and increase dry powder to purchase high quality stocks and increase core positions on significant pullbacks.

3. Rotate away from the irrationally overbought areas in the market like gold and silver at that time, ultra high flyers, high alpha stocks, and digital assets which typically experience some of the most considerable declines during correction periods. Moreover, I rotated into some badly beaten down segments like undervalued oil and gas, certain nuclear stocks, and other "safer haven" areas in the market.

4. We discussed implementing various hedging strategies like selling covered calls, collars, and other implementing other hedging mechanisms.

5. We also discussed capitalizing on potential downside in major market averages and individual stocks by implementing more aggressive "hedging strategies," such as implementing put options, purchasing inverse ETFs, and even shorting certain individual stocks as well as futures contracts (my preferred way to "hedge").

The S&P 500 "SPX"

We've witnessed about an 8% pullback in the SPX from its peak in late January so far. The million dollar question is whether the pullback is close to over, or could we see a more dramatic correction event?

By more dramatic, I mean a potential test of the 6,300 support level (10% correction), and in a worse-case, a possible decline to the 6,200-6,000 range (12-15% correction). In my view, these are still all viable marks. So then, in a bullish case, we may have seen the bottom at around 6,500, in a base case, the bottom may materialize around 6,300, and in a bearish case we could see the SPX at about 6,000 if the selloff really becomes bad. I believe it's premature to discuss probabilities of a bear market, as we would likely need considerable economic deterioration, and such an event may still be 1-2 years away. There's also a possibility of a black swan event materializing, but the is not really something that we can predict.

Based on the current circumstances and market dynamics, I believe that there is about a 25% probability that the S&P 500 may have already bottomed, roughly a 50% probability that the SPX may test around the 6,400-6,300 support 10% correction range, and about a 25% possibility that the SPX may decline as low as 6,200-6,000 before this correctional phase ends.

Technically Speaking

We've seen negative RSI divergences for quite some time. Therefore, it was only a matter of time until the correction materialized and a considerable pullback occurred. However, the RSI hit nearly 30 recently, which is an abnormally low point. Still, it's troubling me that the SPX is below the 200 day moving average here, and if it doesn't rebound back above it swiftly, the path of least resistance, which still appears lower, may prevail.

While it's not necessary, it would be preferable to see somewhat of a washout process, during which we see some sort of panic selling, to confirm a capitulation bottom event. The declines have been very "orderly" thus far, and I find this to be a troubling signal, implying that another wave of higher volatility is still lit to come.

The Nasdaq Composite Index

The Nasdaq has also taken it on the chin, with the tech heavy composite dropping by approximately 10% from its recent high point. We also see a very negative RSI divergence with a double top pattern. We even have the 50 day MA sloping lower, dangerously close to the 200 day MA, suggesting that the dreaded "death cross" could be near, unless the price action improves dramatically, and quickly.

Stocks Close To Buy-In Levels

I don't think it's necessary to go through all of the big tech stock charts here, but let's just look at some of the "MAG" stocks' recent peak to trough declines:

  • Apple (AAPL): 15%
  • Microsoft (MSFT): 33%
  • Nvidia (NVDA): 20%
  • Alphabet (GOOG, GOOGL): 17%
  • Amazon (AMZN): 24%
  • Meta Platforms (META): 27%
  • Tesla (TSLA): 29%
  • Broadcom (AVGO): 28%
  • Oracle (ORCL): 60%
  • AMD (AMD): 32%
  • Average Decline: 28.5%.

With an average decline of roughly 28.5%, the "MAG," leading AI tech segment stocks have had considerable pullbacks, corrections, and even an essential crash in stocks like Oracle and even Microsoft, dropping by 60% and 33% respectively in relatively short timeframes.

Significant Valuation Resets

Now, here are the current forward P/E and forward PEG valuations (according to my EPS estimates and my proprietary PEG formula) for the underlying mega tech stocks. Please, have a look for yourself, and then tell me if you still believe that the U.S. tech segment is overvalued or not.

  • Apple (AAPL): Forward P/E ratio 25-27, forward PEG ratio 1.85. Apple still appears relatively costly here, which is why I am not buyer of this stock here. Apple gets the high-end hardware and services business premium, but I am not convinced its stock should be valued this highly. It may have limited upside potential relative to many other companies.
  • Microsoft (MSFT): Forward P/E ratio 18-20, forward PEG ratio 1.1. I would argue that a 1.1 PEG ratio for Microsoft is not expensive, and it's the cheapest the stock has been in a very long time. There are some extensive spending and IA risks involved, but I am considering repurchasing Microsoft around these levels or slightly lower.
  • Nvidia (NVDA): Forward P/E ratio is about 15, forward PEG ratio is roughly 0.75. Nvidia appears cheap here from a valuation perspective and if the AI train does not derail, this stock could potentially have another significant wave higher, in my view. I plan to increase to my relatively small/reduced Nvidia position as the market stabilizes.
  • Alphabet (GOOG, GOOGL): Forward P/E ratio 20-22, forward PEG ratio 1.17. Google is in a unique position because of its dominant position in search and the remarkable success it's achieved with Gemini, its AI program, and other business segments. While Alphabet appears relatively inexpensive here, I am not too fond of its technical image, and may consider reentering on a more considerable pullback to the $270-250 range.
  • Amazon (AMZN): Forward P/E ratio 22, forward PEG ratio about 1. Roughly a 1 PEG ratio is relatively inexpensive for a dominant market leading growth company like Amazon. Therefore, will look to reenter this stock as the market turbulence calms down and the dust settles from this pullback process.
  • Meta Platforms (META): Forward P/E ratio 16, forward PEG ratio 0.9. A PEG ratio below 1 and a forward P/E ratio of about 16 are very low for a company like Meta. I believe this stock is a buy at these levels and if it goes lower. I plan to increase my Meta position if there is further weakness in this stock.
  • Tesla (TSLA): Forward P/E ratio 60-70, forward PEG ratio is about 1-1.2. I am implementing higher end EPS estimates for next year to derive the forward P/E and PEG ratios. While Tesla may appear expensive on a P/E basis, its low PEG rate suggests the stock may be priced relatively inexpensively given the potential for very considerable EPS growth in future years. Due to Tesla's enormous intermediate and longer term potential, I have a substantial position in Tesla's stock.
  • Broadcom (AVGO): Forward P/E ratio about 18, forward PEG ratio is about 0.55. Broadcom actually appears very cheap from a forward P/E and forward PEG ratio perspective. However, the technicals have worsened recently which gives me some caution. Still, if the AI growth remains robust Broadcom may still be a very solid buy here. I plan to reenter this stock if the technical image holds up or if there is a better buying opportunity at lower levels.
  • Oracle (ORCL): Forward P/E ratio is about 19, forward PEG ratio is roughly 0.8. Oracle appears quite cheap as the stock has been sold off irrationally low in my view and its PEG ratio is very low. Oracle may achieve higher than expected growth in future years, and I believe there is considerable upside ahead. Therefore, I own a sizable position in Oracle's stock.
  • Advanced Micro Devices (AMD): Forward P/E ratio 18-20, forward PEG ratio is roughly 0.5. AMD may actually be the cheapest stock here with a forward PEG ratio of only about 0.5. Therefore, I have a considerable position in AMD and plan to increase, especially if there is further downside ahead.

The Market May Be Too Bearish On The Fed Now

Where did all the rate cut probabilities go? What happened to the September rate cut? Only several weeks ago there was still about an 80% or so probability that we would see at least one cut by the September FOMC event. Now, the market is projecting zero rate cuts for the whole year. Moreover, there is now a much higher probability for a rate hike than a rate cut before the year runs out. This potentially much more hawkish interpretation of the Fed's interest rate hike trajectory has weighed heavily on sentiment in the near term.

However, we must remember that these are only projections, and nothing is written in stone. In fact, I believe the Fed Chair sounded very uncertain about many things during his post-FOMC press conference. It sounded like the Fed was uncertain about the labor market, inflation, potential impacts of higher oil, tariffs, and the war. It also seemed like J. Powell wanted to say that the "oil shock" should be transitory, but it also seemed like he did not want to use the "T word."

In any case, just as the rate cut pendulum appears to have swung too much in the hawkish direction, there is a high probability that we may see the potential for more rate cut decreases as the war winds down and oil prices moderate.

The Bottom Line: In Trump We Trust

President Trump said that they were working on a deal with Iran and that Iran even offered the U.S. a present. I am very curious as to what this "present" may be. Of course, this was after a multi-week relentless bombing campaign, that has crippled the Iranian leadership, armed forces, and has degraded much of its capabilities to defend itself effectively. We also know that if a deal is not reached to reopen the Strait of Hormuz the next move may involve intense bombing of Iran's energy infrastructure. Furthermore, this could escalate to having to put boots on the ground in some Iranian territories, like Kharg Island.

Clearly, all of the military hardware buildup in the surrounding region is suggesting that President Trump is preparing for "partial invasion," (if necessary). This is something that any remaining leadership in Iran does not want. Losing Kharg Island could equate to losing 90% of Iran's oil exporting abilities, and they could also lose their stranglehold on the Strait of Hormuz. Despite the potential for more near term volatility, the likeliest outcome may be that once the hostilities end, the U.S. is in a much more advantageous position geopolitically and economically. Therefore, I remain constructive on U.S. markets, and high quality U.S. stocks in particular, and I maintain my 2026 year-end S&P 500 target at 7,800 (8,000 bullish-case).

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: JEPI Vs. SPY: The Case For Covered Calls In 3 Charts
data.description:
SPY vs. JEPI: Current Valuation Gap and VIX Unlikely to Last

My last work on the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPI) was published on Dec 23, 2025, and rated the stock as a buy. The rating was mainly based on the consideration of the VIX index (which was relatively low at that time), the inflation data, and also JEPI’s valuation. Since the publication of that work, our macroeconomic conditions have undergone a few material changes, which motivated this reassessment of the return/risk calculus of JEPI relative to the broader market, for which I will use the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY) as a proxy. More specifically, the remainder of this article will examine 2 changes in detail: the changes in the VIX index and also the changes in their dividends.

In the end, my overall conclusion is that the current market conditions present a compelling case for JEPI due to its high income and volatility-mitigating strategies. As a reflection of the ongoing volatility, both funds have suffered sharp price corrections since early March, as you can see from the next chart. Looking ahead, with the uncertainties evolving around inflation, interest rates, and also geological conflicts in the Middle East, I anticipate elevated VIX to persist. Under this outlook, JEPI offers a superior risk-adjusted profile for the current environment, especially for investors who need active income, as detailed next.

JEPI and SPY: Basic Information

Before diving in, let me quickly cover the basics of JEPI and SPY. The SPY ETF probably needs no introduction, and I will just quote the fund’s brief bio below.

SPY Fund description: The fund tracks the massively popular US index, the S&P 500. The index offers outstanding exposure to the US large-cap space.

JEPI needs a bit more of an introduction. The fund actively evaluates overvalued and undervalued stocks in the SP500 universe to construct its portfolio. In the meantime, the fund also actively sells options on its holdings to provide high income and also mitigate volatility, as detailed below:

JEPI fund description: Seeks to generate high income through a combination of selling options and investing in U.S. large cap stocks. Constructs a diversified, low volatility equity portfolio through proprietary research designed to identify overvalued and undervalued stocks with attractive risk/return characteristics. Seeks to deliver a significant portion of the returns associated with the S&P 500 Index with less volatility, in addition to monthly income.

Due to JEPI’s above strategy, its holdings are more diversified than SPY, whose holdings are dominated by a handful of technology mega caps. As shown in the next chart, SPY’s largest holdings are dominated by these stocks, including Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) (7.79%), Apple Inc. (AAPL) (6.61%), Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) (5.23%), etc. In contrast, Nvidia is the only mega-cap stock in JEPI’s top 10 holdings, and its weight is also relatively low at 1.72% only.

With this background, the next sections will explore the investment implications of JEPI and SPY’s different strategies.

Chart 1: Avoid Loss vs. Achieve Gain

With SPY’s more concentrated bets on high-quality and growth-oriented stocks, it remains the gold standard for long-term capital appreciation in my view. JEPI’s use of options effectively puts a cap on the appreciation potential in the long run. JEPI’s fees are also higher than SPY (0.35% vs. 0.09%), creating a larger long-term fee drag.

However, for investors who need current income, the risk calculus can be very different. If you recall from the chart in the first section, JEPI has outperformed SPY by about 4.73% in total return since my last JEPI article; the alpha the most analysts quote is shown as case 0 in the table below. But there are a few other ways to calculate the alpha that can be more relevant depending on your circumstance. The detailed definitions of these alphas are provided in an earlier article, and I will just quote the end results here:

Case 1 shows the so-called break-even alpha. A 2.18% loss needs a 2.22% (not 2.18%) rally to break even –due to the asymmetry of compounding. So, in this case, the alpha would be 4.77%, larger than the 4.18% in case 0. Case 2 shows the case of withdrawal. If you start with $100 and withdraw 5% when you are 2.18% underwater, you will be left with $92.82 in the portfolio after the withdrawal. Then you will need a 7.73% rally for your nest egg to grow back to $100. But if you withdraw 5% when your portfolio gained 2.55%, you will only need a 2.51% rally for the nest egg to grow back, resulting in an alpha of 5.22%.

Chart 2: SPY and JEPI Dividend Yield

Since my last analysis, both SPY and JEPI have announced their new dividends. As shown in the next table, JEPI’s latest declarations showed a healthy YOY growth of 7.2% and 5.6%, outpacing inflation by a good margin. With these new declarations, JEPI’s TTM dividend yield stands at 8.49%, which is nearly eight times the TTM yield of SPY of 1.14%, as shown in the second chart below.

With such a large difference, the alpha considerations presented in the foregoing section further change. When you have a sufficiently large nest egg and the high yield from JEPI is sufficient to cover your ongoing needs, you don’t need to actively sell JEPI shares anymore. This could shield you from the price fluctuations, which are of particular relevance with the ongoing market volatility (more on this in a minute). These fluctuations are random and don’t care when we need money. Selling shares at the wrong times is the true danger for our long-term financial health, in our experience.

Before leaving this chart, also note that both JEPI's and SPY’s current yields are below their historical average, indicating some valuation premium for both funds. To wit, JEPI has a 4-year average yield of 8.88%, and SPY’s 4-year average yield is 1.34%. Currently, JEPI's TTM yield is 4.6% below its 4-year average, suggesting a milder premium compared to SPY, whose current TTM yield is about 15% below its 4-year average.

Chart 3: SPY’s Implied Volatility

Due to macroscopic risks mentioned earlier (inflation, interest rates, geological conflicts, etc.), the implied volatility (IV) underlying SPY is quite elevated, as you can tell from the following statistics. To wit, SPY’s current IV is 19.9. This puts it in the 88th percentile in the past 52 weeks. In other words, the IV was higher than the current level only 12% of the time. Also, note that SPY’s current IV is also well above the HV (historical volatility) by 17.8%.

Empirical data (see this study as an example) suggests the correlation between high volatility and poor equity price returns, which would impact both SPY and JEPI. After all, JEPI draws its holdings from the SP500 universe. However, JEPI collects option premium due to its option overlay, and the premium increases together with higher IV readings. This mechanism helps to offset the negative impacts on price returns.

Other Risks and Final Thoughts

Before closing, a few other risks entailed by SPY and especially JEPI are worth mentioning. As aforementioned, both funds are trading at a valuation premium, judging by their dividends. In terms of P/E ratios, JEPI now carries a multiple of 22.16x per its latest disclosure shown below, which actually represents a slight premium over the S&P 500 Index’s multiple of 21.82x (by about 1.5%). As an active fund, JEPI charges a fee (0.35%) that is much higher than SPY (0.09%) and also incurs a much higher turnover rate of 172%. A higher turnover rate tends to cause higher trading frictions. Finally, in a bull market where SPY and its member stocks rally strongly, the options overlay used by JEPI will limit the upside potential and cause JEPI to underperform despite its generous yield. On the positive side, besides higher yield, JEPI also features a much lower beta of 0.36 and a reduced standard deviation of 6.62, compared to a beta of 1.0 and a standard deviation of 10.76 for the SP500 index.

On the topic of taxes, the tax treatment of distributions for JEPI is going to be a mix of ordinary income and qualified dividends, due to its use of equity-linked notes (ELNs) for enhanced income generation. This is different from many covered-call strategies that JEPI is often lumped in with, which often have a significant portion of income taxed as return-of-capital (RoC).

To close, SPY (or other similar funds such as VOO and IVV) certainly remains the gold standard for long-term capital appreciation thanks to the low fees, low churn, and broad representation. However, I see a better return/risk curve for JEPI under the current market conditions. With my outlook for the VIX to remain elevated, I see JEPI as a more attractive candidate due to its high income and volatility-mitigating strategies. These features are particularly timely for investors who actively withdraw from their accounts. For these accounts, the benefits of JEPI can be much more than the notional alpha most analysts quote.

data.title: Holding POET Into Q4 Earnings: The Numbers That Matter
data.description:
Introduction

The Canadian company Poet Technologies Inc. (POET) is in the midst of the transition from a pre-revenue stage to operational expansion and commercialization of its photonic integrated circuits (PICs) and optical modules (800G and 1.6T, with the 3.2T in the development stage), protected by patents. As I explained, its main innovation is the Optical Interposer, which integrates photonic and electronic components on a single chip without wires. The advantages of this solution are lower cost (fewer components are needed) and power efficiency. We immediately understand that we are talking about the intriguing layer of the AI stack. Compute is where NVIDIA and AMD play, while we find POET in the infrastructure and networking layers. This means that POET is not a computer or a software company. Its optical engines move data between AI accelerators at extremely fast speeds (basically, the speed of light). Without this role, GPUs would be a massive bottleneck for AI. With an analogy taken from the human body, NVIDIA plays the role of the brain, while POET produces components that are part of the nervous system.

After developing the technology, POET has secured manufacturing capacity through Globetronics and NationGate in Malaysia. The company also has a partnership with Mitsubishi, Foxconn, and Luxshare, which are suppliers of the hyperscalers.

It may sound obvious, but if POET manages to execute, it could be a real beneficiary of the ongoing data center buildout, which is expected to last, at least, through 2030. As POET shows, the inflection point for 800G demand is already behind us, but 2026 is the ramp year for 1.6T, which should soon see its TAM grow to $1.5B in two years, with the $15B landmark in sight by 2030. To sum it all up, the TAM that POET estimated in its investor presentation has already been revised by 43% for 2026 and by 46% for 2027.

A big variable for POET is CPO (co-packaged optics) growth, which seeks to eliminate pluggable modules from the switch chassis. Now, on one side, POET actually serves the pluggable market, which means that CPO growth could cannibalize it, especially if the hyperscalers decide to skip the 1.6T modules and go to the CPO switches from NVIDIA or Broadcom. But this is a short-term issue because, over the long term, POET's light source products are designed for the architecture that CPO uses: chip-to-chip optical connectivity inside the servers. Since the CPO market is growing extremely fast, many believe that this, per se, validates the fact that POET is trying to solve a real issue and service an explosive market. The issue here is timing. So far, in this field, POET is at the prototype stage, and POET needs demand for 1.6T pluggable modules to hold and grow. If this doesn't happen, then the company may find itself caught in a dangerous gap.

It is hard to predict what will happen, but I think that the data center buildout is such a massive trend in a world where hardware suppliers are more and more constrained that we will see multiple technologies used as long as they are ready for use (by the way, this could lead us to those considerations about the real lifecycle of the AI capex and how it should be properly amortized).

POET's Q4 FY25 Earnings Preview

Before we dive into crunching a few numbers, let me give you a piece of context, in case you are not familiar with the financials of this company. Since we are at a pre-revenue stage, POET's income statement is basically non-existent, as it is more a statement of operations. Therefore, we don't have to focus on the bottom line but on the operating loss and the operating cash. As of Q3 FY25, the reported revenue was $300k, the net loss was $9.4M, but the operating loss was $2.8M.

What stands out is this: revenues have been increasing for five consecutive quarters; the cash burn is improving from where it was a year ago, at around $9M.

Having said that, let's move on to the upcoming Q4 print. The pivotal question, as far as I see it, is whether any product revenue will appear and be recognized. The revenue that the consensus expects is $500k, which may suggest that some initial production may appear. In fact, we know that POET secured a $500k order for 1.6T modules in September 2025, which should have already been delivered in the first months of 2026. Secondly, in October 2025, the company received a $5M order to be shipped in H2 2026. What matters is the first one, and in case we see some product revenue, I believe investors will celebrate. Otherwise, we will have to wait for the Q1 FY26 print, confirming that 2026 will be a make-or-break year, at least for the stock's performance.

If product revenue appears, we can then see for the first time what the company's gross margin looks like. This is of utmost importance because POET has long claimed its competitive cost advantage over other solutions but has not yet given us real numbers to prove it.

Finally, we will need to pay attention to how the CEO will speak about 2026. So far, this year has been described as the revenue ramp year. In this regard, the upcoming report is even more important because, being released at the end of March, it could also shed more light on what has been going on in the first three months of this pivotal year. If we hear talks about shipment schedules and new orders, then we could say that the company has definitely exited the pre-revenue stage.

Valuation

Clearly, when we lack the main financial figures, it is really hard to value the company, as most multiples are either undefined or don't make a lot of sense (for example, the fwd P/sales is above 700). An investor who chooses to buy POET is not seeking earnings power, since the stock, right now, seems closer to an option with a certain degree of probability that the outcome will be in favor of the buyer.

Right now, POET trades at around $6.00, which leads to a market cap of roughly $877M. As a result of a cash position of $92M and total debt below $8M, we have an EV of $791M. This means that the company would need to report $79M in revenue just to lead to a 10x EV/revenue multiple, which is a scenario that not even the bulls imagine. I think it would be a great result if POET ended this year with $10M in revenue. This means the multiple is actually 79.

Let me dig a bit deeper and explain my $10M forecast. In 2026, 1.6T transceiver sales begin, and we said that this could lead to sales of $15B by 2030. POET sells optical engines, which represent a component worth around 20% to 30% of the transceiver value. This means that the TAM for POET could be worth between $3B and $4.5B at the 2030 peak. The revenue, I imagine, means that this year, POET will have a market share of around 0.25%. It is surely not an aggressive hypothesis, but to reach revenues of almost $80M, we would need POET to have a market share above 2%. It is possible, but POET needs the scale, which is probably not the story for 2026 but, more likely, for 2027 through 2030. In this assumption, I consider that light sources are still a long-term project that doesn't have an impact on 2026 revenues.

Conclusion

I think that, compared to a quarter ago, the outlook has improved a bit, and this is why I am upgrading the stock from sell to hold. But I remain on the sidelines because the outcome of this investment relies heavily on how the company will execute. My rule is to let some time pass to test how the company operates. I know I may leave some money on the table, but I have also seen that once one identifies a winner correctly, there is plenty of upside still left.

data.title: Super Micro Computer Faces Immense Reputational Risk
data.description:
Super Micro Computer (SMCI) can’t catch a break.

Following years of highly publicized accounting slip-ups, the company now finds itself in another regulatory quagmire, this time pertaining to the sale of NVIDIA (NVDA) chips to China. Although the company itself has not been accused of any wrongdoing, several of its top executives have been implicated in sales that–if real–are contrary to U.S. regulations. Co-founder Yih-Shyan Liaw was among those named in documents filed by regulators. Liaw’s high profile poses the threat of reputational damage to Super Micro, as Liaw was a decision maker serving on the company's board until a few days ago.

This isn’t the first time that Super Micro Computer has made headlines for questionable reasons. Prior to the NVIDIA controversy, the company was famous for accounting issues, including auditor resignations, delayed statements, and missed deadlines. These issues were severe enough that the company’s stock plunged over the last 12 months despite reporting extremely impressive growth numbers. It’s not all that often that you see a company growing the top line by 34% trading at just 12 times earnings, but when regulatory risk becomes elevated, you can get some strange outcomes.

So, Super Micro Computer investors face a complex situation. The company’s growth numbers are very good; its valuation is cheap on a sector-relative basis; and its margins are respectable. If all of this were “legitimate” then it would be an open and shut case: a slam dunk buy. The problem is twofold:

  1. The company's financials might not be reliable. This is the crux of the problem. Credible reports from the highest levels of the U.S. government have said that the GPU sales now being investigated, were facilitated by unreliable documents and possible deceptions of inspectors. While this matter is technically distinct from the accounting irregularities that were the concern last year, it raises similar questions: unreliable documents and denial of access to key inspectors, are both seen as possible signs of misstatement. This, combined with the previous resignation of SMCI's auditor, casts doubt on the notion that the company's accounting issues have been fully resolved.

  2. There is credible basis for thinking that top SMCI officers are going to be under regulatory scrutiny. This is already the case with co-founder Liaw, who was both a board member and Vice President of Business Development before his recent resignation.

So, while Super Micro Computer stock looks cheap right now, it may be a value trap, particularly if the accounting issues surface again. Speaking of which: the ongoing NVIDIA controversy ties in with the accounting concerns that surfaced in years prior. While technically no new accounting issues have surfaced this year--the last news on the matter having been a clean audit opinion last year--the NVIDIA story casts doubt on whether we’ve heard the last we’ll ever hear on the matter.
